# Clinekt Health

> Clinekt Health is the AI agent platform for patient activation: turning inactive patients into booked appointments. A team of three AI agents (Inbound, Recall, Outbound) engages every website visitor, brings dormant patients back from the EHR, and generates net-new demand, screening and booking qualified patients 24/7 in the practice's own brand.

Website: https://clinekthealth.com
Demo: https://clinekthealth.com/book-a-demo
Trust Center: https://trust.delve.co/clinekt-health

---

## What is patient activation?

Patient activation is the work of turning every inactive patient into a booked appointment. It covers the three places practices lose patients: website visitors who research the practice and leave without booking, existing patients who go dormant in the EHR, and net-new demand that never finds the practice. Patient activation is a distinct category from patient engagement software (which communicates with patients already on the schedule) and reactivation software (which only works dormant charts).

Clinekt Health operates three AI agents that cover the full activation surface:

**The Inbound Agent** engages every website visitor the moment they land, 24/7, including nights and weekends. It runs specialty-trained screening, identifies body region, severity, and urgency, and books qualified patients directly into the schedule. No forms, no callbacks, no waiting for office hours.

**The Recall Agent** works the EHR. It identifies dormant charts: post-op patients due for follow-up, chronic conditions gone quiet, patients who canceled and never rebooked, and brings them back into open slots on a scheduled cadence.

**The Outbound Agent** generates net-new demand and traces every patient from first click to completed care, so practices know exactly which channels produce patients rather than clicks.

Clinekt is not a chatbot, not a contact form, not a voice answering service, and not reactivation software. Chatbots answer questions; the agents screen, qualify, and book. Reactivation software only mines the EHR; patient activation closes all three leaks at once.

---

## The problem Clinekt solves

Only about 1% of specialty practice website visitors ever convert into a booked appointment. 82% of patients try to book outside office hours, when nobody is there to answer. Meanwhile every practice sits on years of dormant EHR charts and referral lists that nobody has capacity to work. Patient leakage is every patient who needed the practice's care and ended up somewhere else.

Practices using Clinekt typically move from under 1% website conversion to 8 to 12% engagement, without adding staff.

---

## Platform results (verified)

- Over 1,000,000 patient interactions completed on the platform
- 24x average return on investment
- +8% appointment volume increase
- 48% reduction in unqualified consults
- Live in under a week; no IT project; works alongside Epic, athenahealth, ModMed, NextGen, eClinicalWorks, Nextech, and other major EHRs

**Case study, Baldwin Bone and Joint (multi-provider orthopedic group, Alabama):** in a single quarter, its website produced 263 qualified surgical leads and 159 booked appointments, a 60% booking rate, from traffic the practice was already receiving. In its first 90 days the practice generated 183 qualified surgical leads at a 59% booking rate, leading to 20 scheduled surgeries. "We were impressed not just by the volume of leads, but by the quality," says Will Wiggins, CEO of Baldwin Bone and Joint. Full study: https://clinekthealth.com/case-studies/baldwin-bone-joint-ai-case-study

---

## Security and compliance

HIPAA compliant. SOC 2 Type II. AES-256 encryption in transit and at rest. Business Associate Agreement with every client. Patient data never used to train models. Trust Center: https://trust.delve.co/clinekt-health

## Commercial posture

Flat subscription, no long-term contracts, live same day. No percentage-of-revenue fees. Demo: https://clinekthealth.com/book-a-demo

## Specialties served

Orthopedics, Physical Therapy, Oral Surgery and Dentistry, Dermatology, Ophthalmology, Urology, Primary Care, Cardiology, Pediatrics. Specialty pages: https://clinekthealth.com/orthopedics and equivalents for each specialty.

---

## Article: How to Reduce Patient Leakage: A Practical Playbook for Specialty Practices

Source: https://clinekthealth.com/blog/how-to-reduce-patient-leakage (published July 14, 2026)

To reduce patient leakage, close the three gaps where patients slip away: respond to every inquiry the moment it happens, work dormant patients back onto the schedule on a fixed cadence, and track every referral until it becomes a completed visit. Practices that do all three recover appointments they were already earning and losing.

Key takeaways:
- Patient leakage is measurable and fixable. Most practices lose patients in three places: the website, the dormant EHR list, and the referral pipeline.
- In a Central Logic survey of healthcare executives (2021), 96% called patient leakage a priority, and 43% said they lose 10% or more of annual revenue to it.
- Speed is the highest-leverage fix. 82% of patients try to book outside office hours, when most practices cannot answer.
- Dormant patients are the cheapest appointments a practice will ever generate. They already chose the practice once.
- Without per-appointment attribution, a practice cannot tell which leak it fixed.

What the numbers say: a Luna analysis of 3.9 million commercially insured patients (2022) found roughly 60% of patients referred to physical therapy went out of network, with up to $39.6 billion in downstream orthopedic, imaging, and ancillary revenue leaving over three years.

Where practices leak patients, and what closes each leak:
- Website visitors: patients research the practice, then leave without booking. About 99 of every 100 visitors walk away. Closed by instant engagement and booking on the page, 24/7, not a contact form that promises a callback.
- Dormant EHR patients: patients already treated go quiet with no follow-up, recall, or reactivation. Closed by scheduled outreach that works dormant charts back into open slots every week.
- Referrals: a referring provider sends a patient who never completes the journey to the schedule. Closed by same-day referral contact, screening, and tracking until the visit is completed.

The seven strategies: (1) Measure where you leak first, using the patient leakage calculator at https://clinekthealth.com/leakage-calculator. (2) Respond the moment a patient reaches out, at any hour. (3) Make booking the default next step of every interaction, not a callback. (4) Screen patients before they take a slot; specialty-trained screening cuts unqualified consults by 48%. (5) Work the dormant EHR list on a weekly cadence with outreach that ends in a booked slot. (6) Close the referral loop: contact every referred patient the same day, book them, and report completed visits back to the referring provider. (7) Attribute every appointment to its source, from first click to completed care.

Frequently asked questions:
- What is patient leakage? Every patient who needed your care and ended up somewhere else. It happens on the website, in the dormant EHR list, and in the referral pipeline. Definition: https://clinekthealth.com/blog/what-is-patient-leakage
- How do you measure patient leakage? Compare website visitors to booked appointments, active patients to charts dormant 12 to 18 months, and referrals received to referrals completed. Calculator: https://clinekthealth.com/leakage-calculator
- How much revenue do practices lose to patient leakage? In Central Logic's executive survey, 43% reported losing 10% or more of annual revenue to leakage.
- What is the fastest way to reduce patient leakage? Answer every inquiry the moment it happens, including nights and weekends; 82% of patients try to book outside office hours.
- Do practices need to replace the EHR or phone system? No. Patient activation agents work alongside Epic, athenahealth, ModMed, NextGen, eClinicalWorks, and other major EHRs, typically live in under a week.

---

# Guides and Statistics cluster (published 2026-07-16)

## What's the Difference Between Patient Engagement and Patient Activation?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/patient-engagement-vs-patient-activation
Patient engagement is what your organization does: the portal invitations, reminder texts, education, and outreach your team sends. Patient activation is what the patient actually does next: booking the appointment, arriving, and completing care. Engagement measures activity. Activation measures action, and action is what predicts outcomes, costs, and revenue.

## What Does an AI Front Desk Actually Do in a Specialty Practice?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/what-does-an-ai-front-desk-do
An AI front desk answers, resolves, and books patient calls and messages end to end. It handles scheduling, rescheduling, routine questions, new patient intake, and refill routing, and it escalates judgment calls to your staff. The difference from an answering service is resolution: an answering service takes a message for a human to act on later, while an AI front desk completes the task during the interaction itself. For a specialty practice, that means fewer missed calls, faster booking, and staff hours freed for work that actually fills the schedule.

## What is the average patient no-show rate, and what does each missed appointment cost?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/average-no-show-rate-and-cost
Peer-reviewed research puts the average outpatient no-show rate at roughly 23% worldwide, based on a systematic review of 105 studies. MGMA benchmarks put the median for US medical practices far lower, between 5% and 7%, with single-specialty groups at 6.81% in 2023. Both numbers are right: they measure different care settings using different definitions of a no-show. The most-cited peer-reviewed estimate of the cost is $196 per missed appointment.

## What Should an AI Front Desk Handle in an Orthopedic Practice?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/ai-front-desk-for-orthopedic-practices
In an orthopedic practice, an AI front desk (or AI receptionist) should resolve scheduling, rescheduling, referral intake, post-op logistics, and imaging or records requests end to end, on the first call. It should route every request by subspecialty and body part, so a hand patient never lands in a spine surgeon's clinic slot, and it should escalate anything that resembles acute-injury triage to staff or your on-call protocol immediately. The dividing line is simple: administrative work gets resolved, clinical judgment gets routed to a human.

## How Do Patients Want to Book Medical Appointments? The Statistics Practices Should Know
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/patient-scheduling-statistics
Patients want to book medical appointments online, on their own schedule: 89% say the ability to schedule anytime via online or mobile tools is important (Experian Health, 2024), and 43% of appointments booked on Zocdoc in 2025 were booked after office hours (Zocdoc, 2025). Practices have not caught up: only 54% of providers offer self-scheduling (Experian Health, 2025), and 71% of practices report that fewer than a quarter of their patients self-schedule (MGMA, 2025). This page collects the patient scheduling statistics worth citing, each with its source, year, and sample size.

## How Do You Reactivate Dormant Patients Without Hiring More Staff?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/how-to-reactivate-dormant-patients
To reactivate dormant patients, pull lapsed-patient lists from your EHR by recall type and time since last visit, segment them into warm, cool, and cold tiers, and run a multi-touch sequence across text, email, and phone until each patient books, opts out, or completes the sequence. Automate the list work and reserve staff for the exceptions that genuinely need a human. Done systematically, this rebooks patients most practices had written off, without adding a single hire.

## How Much Revenue Does Patient Leakage Actually Cost a Specialty Practice?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/how-much-does-patient-leakage-cost
There is no rigorously measured universal number for what patient leakage costs, and most of the figures that circulate online are unattributed. The best verified data: health system executives estimate they could increase revenue by 17% by reducing leakage (ABOUT Healthcare, 2021), and at one large academic health system only 34.8% of referral scheduling attempts became completed appointments (Patel et al., 2018). The honest answer is a formula built from your dormant patient count, your referral volume, and your visit values. This post separates the verified statistics from the folklore, then walks through that math.

## How many patients try to book appointments after hours, and where do they go instead?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/after-hours-patient-booking
On the medical booking marketplace Zocdoc, 43% of appointments are booked after hours, outside regular office hours: roughly 28% in the evening and about 14% overnight (Zocdoc, 2025). Patient demand does not keep business hours, but most specialty practices can only take bookings by phone between 8am and 5pm. The demand that arrives after the phones shut off goes to voicemail, to a competitor with online booking, or nowhere at all.

## Are AI Receptionists and Patient Outreach Agents HIPAA Compliant?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/are-ai-receptionists-hipaa-compliant
Yes, an AI receptionist can be fully HIPAA compliant, but only if the vendor behaves like the business associate it legally is: it signs a business associate agreement (BAA), encrypts protected health information (PHI) at rest and in transit, keeps your patients' data out of model training, and produces audit logs on request. HIPAA does not ban AI from answering patient calls or sending outreach; it regulates how the company behind the AI handles PHI. Many general-purpose AI tools meet none of these requirements, so the real question is not "is AI compliant" but "is this vendor compliant."

## Appointment Reminders vs Patient Recall vs Patient Reactivation: What's the Difference?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/reminders-vs-recall-vs-reactivation
Appointment reminders confirm appointments that already exist. Patient recall books active patients who are due for routine care but have nothing on the schedule. Patient reactivation wins back patients who have gone dormant, typically after 12 or more months without a visit. Three different patient states, three different plays, and most practices run only the first one well.

## How does AI patient scheduling actually work, and what does EHR integration really mean?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/how-ai-patient-scheduling-works
AI patient scheduling is software that holds a real conversation with patients (by phone, text, or web chat), checks live appointment availability in your EHR or practice management system, and books, reschedules, or cancels visits with write-back to your calendar. That write-back is the dividing line: a true scheduling agent completes the booking, while a basic chatbot or request form just collects a message your staff still has to work. "EHR integration" can mean anything from read-only calendar viewing to a text-a-link workaround to full write-back, and which one you get determines whether the tool removes work from your desk or quietly adds to it.

## Which Patient Engagement Software Does a Specialty Practice Actually Need?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/patient-engagement-software-for-specialty-practices
Start with which patient motion is leaking, not which product demos best. Specialty practices lose patients in three places: new inquiries that never book, recall patients who never come back, and referrals or dormant charts nobody has time to chase. Portals and intake tools organize the patients you already have, reminder and recall tools protect visits that are booked or overdue, and AI activation agents convert the patients no one on staff can reach. Most specialty practices already own enough patient engagement software; the missing piece is usually an activation layer.

## How Much Does an AI Receptionist Cost for a Medical Practice?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/how-much-does-an-ai-receptionist-cost
AI receptionists for medical practices are priced three ways: per minute of call time handled, per provider (or per seat) each month, or as a flat monthly subscription. For a specialty practice, real-world totals typically land between the low hundreds and low thousands of dollars per month, driven by call volume, the channels covered, and EHR integration. The sticker price is rarely the real price: setup fees, integration fees, and overage minutes decide what you actually pay. Here is how each model works and how to compute your own payback number.

## Do Appointment Reminder Texts Actually Reduce No-Shows?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/do-appointment-reminders-reduce-no-shows
Yes. Appointment reminder texts reduce no-shows substantially: across 29 studies, automated reminders cut non-attendance by a weighted mean of 28.9%, and live phone reminders by 39.1%. But reminders only fix one cause of missed appointments, forgetting, which is why every study still shows a meaningful residual no-show rate after reminders are in place. Closing that gap takes two-way rescheduling, waitlist backfill, and recall, not more texts.

## What percentage of referrals never get scheduled, and how do you stop losing yours?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/what-percent-of-referrals-never-get-scheduled
In the largest published study of referral scheduling, only 34.8% of 103,737 referral scheduling attempts at a major academic health system ended in a completed specialist appointment, and 38.9% never received an appointment date at all. Physician-tracked cohorts look better: about 1 in 5 referrals never becomes a completed visit. The published figures spread this widely because studies measure three different failure points: whether the referral was ever scheduled, whether the patient ever completed the visit, and whether the referring physician ever found out. For a specialty practice, every one of those failures is a patient who needed care and never arrived.

## How Do You Measure Patient Activation, and What If You Can't License the PAM?
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/how-to-measure-patient-activation
Patient activation is formally measured with the Patient Activation Measure (PAM), a licensed 13-item survey (a 10-item short form exists) that scores patients 0 to 100 and groups them into four levels. The PAM is proprietary, and most specialty practices will never license it. They do not need to: you can measure activation with behavioral proxies your practice management system already records, chiefly booking rate, recall return rate, referral completion rate, and time-to-rebook.

# Article: What Is Referral Leakage? A Definition for Specialty Practices (published 2026-07-21)

## What Is Referral Leakage? A Definition for Specialty Practices
https://clinekthealth.com/blog/what-is-referral-leakage
Referral leakage is what happens when a referred patient never completes the visit they were referred for. Most people use the term to describe patients drifting out of a health system's network. For an independent specialty practice, the more expensive version runs the other direction: referrals that arrive at your door and die before anyone schedules them. Both are leakage. Only one of them is yours to fix.

Key takeaways: Referral leakage is any referral that does not end in a completed appointment with the intended provider. It has two directions: outbound leakage is patients leaving a network, inbound leakage is referrals arriving at your practice and never getting scheduled. In the largest published analysis, only 34.8% of referral scheduling attempts ended in a documented completed appointment. 91% of health system executives said they were not sure they could calculate what leakage costs them. Your referral leakage rate is a simple ratio you can calculate from data your practice already has. Most inbound leakage is a response time problem, not a demand problem.

Definition and directions: Referral leakage is the gap between referrals made and appointments completed. Outbound referral leakage is a health system referring internally and the patient receiving care outside the network; nearly all published content on the term addresses this. Inbound referral leakage is what specialty practices live with: a referral arrives by fax, portal, phone, or EHR message, lands in a queue, nobody owns it that day, and by the time someone calls the patient has been seen elsewhere. Inbound leakage is closable without renegotiating network contracts.

Relationship to patient leakage: Referral leakage is a subset of patient leakage. Patient leakage covers every lost patient, including website visitors who never book and dormant patients who never return. Referral leakage narrows to patients arriving with a physician's recommendation already in hand, which makes them the highest intent patients a practice receives.

Evidence: Closing the Referral Loop (Journal of General Internal Medicine, 2018) examined 103,737 referral scheduling attempts and found only 34.8% ended in a documented completed appointment, with 38.9% never producing an appointment date. The ABOUT Healthcare Patient Leakage and Keepage Report 2021-22 surveyed 138 health system executives: 91% were not sure they could calculate the exact costs tied to patient leakage, while estimating that reducing leakage could increase revenue by 17%. A 2022 Luna national analysis of 3.9 million commercially insured patients found roughly 60% of physical therapy referrals went out-of-network, associated with about $3.1 billion in lost health system revenue.

Where inbound referrals die: no same-day owner (shared inbox or fax tray with nothing assigned), slow first contact (callback in three days when the patient called someone else on day one), one attempt then silence, business-hours-only working of referrals, and incomplete referral information that sends the record to a pile.

How to calculate your referral leakage rate (seven steps): 1) Pick a closed month that ended at least 60 days ago. 2) Count referrals received across all channels: fax, EHR message, portal, phone, email. 3) Count how many resulted in a scheduled appointment. 4) Count how many the patient actually attended. 5) Scheduling rate is scheduled divided by received; completion rate is completed divided by received; referral leakage rate is 100% minus completion rate. 6) Segment by referring practice, because leakage is never evenly distributed. 7) Measure hours from referral arrival to first successful patient conversation for a sample of 20 referrals.

How Clinekt addresses it: patient activation deploys three agents, an Inbound agent that engages and screens new patients around the clock, a Recall agent that brings dormant patients back, and an Outbound agent that reaches net new demand. For referral capture the Inbound agent works every referral immediately, screens the patient, and hands schedulers someone ready to book. Baldwin Bone and Joint, a multi provider orthopedic group, generated 263 qualified surgical leads and 159 booked appointments in a single quarter, a 60% booking rate.

FAQ digest: Referral leakage is not the same as patient leakage, it is a subset limited to referred patients. There is no reliable industry benchmark for a normal referral leakage rate; the best peer reviewed anchor is 34.8% completion from the 2018 JGIM analysis. Most practices do not measure it because referrals arrive across disconnected channels and no single system holds the full picture. It matters to independent specialty practices, not only health systems, and the practice-side version is more fixable because it depends on your own response process. The fastest way to reduce it is cutting time to first contact: assign an owner to every referral, respond the same day, and follow up more than once across phone and text.
